Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## UNSUPERVISED VIDEO DECOMPOSITION USING - SPATIO TEMPORAL ITERATIVE INFERENCE

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Unsupervised multi-object scene decomposition is a fast-emerging problem in representation learning. Despite significant progress in static scenes, such models are unable to leverage important dynamic cues present in video. We propose a novel spatio-temporal iterative inference framework that is powerful enough to jointly model complex multi-object representations and explicit temporal dependencies between latent variables across frames. This is achieved by leveraging 2D-LSTM, temporally conditioned inference and generation within the iterative amortized inference for posterior refinement. Our method improves the overall quality of decompositions, encodes information about the objects’ dynamics, and can be used to predict trajectories of each object separately. Additionally, we show that our model has a high accuracy even without color information. We demonstrate the decomposition, segmentation, and prediction capabilities of our model and show that it outperforms the state-of-the-art on several benchmark datasets, one of which was curated for this work and will be made publicly available.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Unsupervised representation learning, which has a long history dating back to Boltzman Machines (Hinton & Sejnowski, 1986) and original works of Marr (1970), has recently emerged as one of the important directions of research, carrying the newfound promise of alleviating the need for excessively large and fully labeled datasets. More traditional representation learning approaches focus on unsupervised ( _e.g._ , autoencoder-based (Pathak et al., 2016; Vincent et al., 2008)) or selfsupervised (Noroozi & Favaro, 2016; Vondrick et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016) learning of _holistic_ representations that, for example, are tasked with producing (spatial (Noroozi & Favaro, 2016), temporal (Vondrick et al., 2016), or color (Zhang et al., 2016)) encodings of images or patches. The latest and most successful methods along these lines include ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019) and others (Sun et al., 2019; Tan & Bansal, 2019) that utilize powerful transformer architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017) coupled with proxy multi-modal tasks ( _e.g._ , masked token prediction or visua-lingual alignment). Learning of good _disentangled_ , spatially _granular_ , representations that are, for example, able to decouple object appearance and shape in complex visual scenes consisting of multiple moving objects remains elusive.

Recent works that attempt to address this challenge can be characterized as: (i) attention-based methods (Crawford & Pineau, 2019b; Eslami et al., 2016), which infer latent representations for each object in a scene, and (ii) iterative refinement models (Greff et al., 2019; 2017), which decompose a scene into a collection of components by grouping pixels. Importantly, the former have been limited to latent representations at object- or image patch-levels, while the latter class of models have illustrated the ability for more granular latent representations at the pixel (segmentation)-level. Specifically, most refinement models learn pixel-level generative models driven by spatial mixtures (Greff et al., 2017) and utilize amortized iterative refinements (Marino et al., 2018) for inference of disentangled latent representations within the VAE framework (Kingma & Welling, 2014); a prime example is IODINE (Greff et al., 2019). However, while providing a powerful model and abstraction which is able to segment and disentangle complex scenes, IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) and other similar architectures are fundamentally limited by the fact that they only consider images. Even when applied for inference in video, they process one frame at a time. This makes it excessively challenging to discover and represent individual instances of objects that may share properties such as appearance and shape but differ in dynamics.

Figure 1: **Unsupervised Video Decomposition.** Our approach allows to infer precise segmentations of the objects via interpretable latent representations, that can be used to decompose each frame and simulate the future dynamics, all in unsupervised fashion. Whenever a new object emerges into a frame the model dynamical adapts and uses one of the segmentation slots to assign to the new object.

In computer vision, it has been a long-held belief that motion carries important information for segmenting objects (Jepson et al., 2002; Weiss & Adelson, 1996). Armed with this intuition, we propose a spatio-temporal amortized inference model capable of not only unsupervised multi-object scene decomposition, but also of learning and leveraging the implicit probabilistic dynamics of each object from perspective raw video alone. This is achieved by introducing temporal dependencies between the latent variables across time. As such, IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) could be considered a special (spatial) case of our spatio-temporal formulation. Modeling temporal dependencies among video frames also allows us to make use of conditional priors (Chung et al., 2015) for variational inference, leading to more accurate and efficient inference results.

The resulting model, illustrated in Fig. 1, achieves superior performance on complex multi-object benchmark datasets (Bouncing Balls and CLEVRER) with respect to state-of-the-art models, including R-NEM (Van Steenkiste et al., 2018) and IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) in terms of segmentation, prediction, and generalization. Our model has a number of appealing properties, including temporal extrapolation, computational efficiency, and the ability to work with complex data exhibiting non-linear dynamics, colors, and changing number of objects within the same video sequence. In addition, we introduce an entropy prior to improve our model’s performance in scenarios where object appearance alone is not sufficiently distinctive ( _e.g._ , greyscale data).

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Unsupervised Scene Representation Learning.** Unsupervised scene representation learning can generally be divided into two groups: attention-based methods, which infer latent representations for each object in a scene, and more complex and powerful iterative refinement models, which often make use of spatial mixtures and can decompose a scene into a collection of estimated components by grouping pixels together. _Attention-based_ methods, such as AIR (Eslami et al., 2016) (Xu et al., 2019) and SPAIR (Crawford & Pineau, 2019b), decompose scenes into latent variables representing the appearance, position, and size of the underlying objects. However, both methods can only infer the objects’ bounding boxes and have not been shown to work on non-trivial 3D scenes with perspective distortions and occlusions. MoNet (Burgess et al., 2019) is the first model in this family tackling more complex data and inferring representations that can be used for instance segmentation of objects. On the other hand, it is not a probabilistic generative model and thus not suitable for density estimation. GENESIS (Engelcke et al., 2020) extends it and alleviates some of its limitations by introducing a probabilistic framework and allowing for spatial relations between the objects. DDPAE (Hsieh et al., 2018) is a framework that uses structured probabilistic models to decompose a video into low-dimensional temporal dynamics with the sole purpose of prediction. It is shown to operate on binary scenes with no perspective distortion and is not capable of generating per-object segmentation masks. _Iterative refinement_ models started with Tagger (Greff et al., 2016) that reasons about the segmentation of its inputs. However, it does not allow explicit latent representations and cannot be scaled to more complex images. NEM (Greff et al., 2017), as an extension of Tagger, uses a spatial mixture model inside an expectation maximization framework, but is limited to binary data. Finally, IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) is a notable example of a model employing iterative amortized inference w.r.t. a spatial mixture formulation and achieves state-of-the-art performance in scene decomposition and segmentation.
**Unsupervised Video Tracking and Object Detection.** SQAIR (Kosiorek et al., 2018), SILOT (Crawford & Pineau, 2019a) and SCALOR (Jiang et al., 2020) are temporal extensions

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

of the static attention-based models that are tailored to tracking and object detection tasks. SQAIR is restricted to binary data and operates at the level of bounding boxes. SILOT and SCALOR are more expressive and can cope with cluttered scenes, a larger numbers of objects, and dynamic backgrounds, but do not work on colored perspective[1] data; accurate segmentation remains a challenge.

**Unsupervised Video Decomposition and Segmentation.** Models employing spatial mixtures and iterative inference in a temporal setting are closest to our method from a technical perspective. Notably, there are only few models falling into this line of work: RTagger (Prémont-Schwarz et al., 2017) is a recurrent extension of Tagger and has same limitations as its predecessor. R-NEM (Van Steenkiste et al., 2018) effectively learns the objects’ dynamics and interactions through a relational module and can produce segmentations but is limited to orthographic binary data.

**Methods without Latent Modeling.** GAN-based ReDO (Chen et al., 2019) uses a model built around the assumption that object regions are independent, guiding the generator by drawing objects’ pixel regions separately and composing them after segmentation. Another model (Arandjelovi´c & Zisserman, 2019) employs the same principles but guide the generator by copying a region of an image into another one. Both architectures are shown to operate on static images only and do not have a clearly interpretable latent space or prediction capabilities.

Our method allows an effective use of temporal information in object-centric decompositions of colored video data. This places our approach between methods like R-NEM, which strictly operates on binary data, and IODINE, whose usage of temporal information is ad-hoc and produces results of limited quality (Table 1). In practice, we leverage a 2D-LSTM and employ an implicit modeling of dynamics by incorporating the hidden states into a conditional prior in the efficient runtime manner.

## 3 DYNAMIC VIDEO DECOMPOSITION

We now introduce our dynamic model for unsupervised video decomposition. Our approach builds upon a generative model of multi-object representations and leverages elements of iterative amortized inference. We briefly review both concepts (§3.1) and then introduce our model (§3.2).

## 3.2 SPATIO-TEMPORAL ITERATIVE INFERENCE

Our proposed model builds upon the concepts introduced in the previous section and enables robust learning of dynamic scenes through spatio-temporal iterative inference. Specifically, we consider the task of decomposing a video sequence **x** = ( **x** _t_ ) _[T] t_ =1[= (] _[x][t,i]_[)] _[T,D] t,i_ =1[into] _[ K]_[slot sequences][ (] **[m]**[(] _t[k]_[)] ) _t_

and _K_ appearance sequences ( _**µ**_[(] _t[k]_[)] ) _t_ . To this end, we introduce explicit temporal dependencies into the sequence of posterior refinements and show how to leverage this contextual information during decoding with a generative model. The resulting computation graph can be thought of as a 2D grid with time dimension _t_ and refinement dimension _r_ (Fig. 2a). Propagation of information along these two axes is achieved with a 2D-LSTM (Graves et al., 2007) (Fig. 2b), which allows us to model the joint probability over the entire video sequence inside the iterative amortized inference framework. The proposed method is expressive enough to model the multimodality of our image formation process and posterior, yet its runtime complexity is smaller than that of its static counterpart.

## 3.2.1 VARIATIONAL OBJECTIVE

Since exact likelihood training is intractable, we formulate our task in terms of a variational objective. In contrast to traditional optimization of the evidence lower bound (ELBO) through static encodings of the approximate posterior, we incorporate information from two dynamic axes: (1) variational estimates from previous refinement steps; (2) temporal information from previous frames. Together, they form the basis for spatio-temporal variational inference via iterative refinements. Specifically, we train our model by maximizing the following ELBO objective[2] :

where the first term expresses the reconstruction error of a single frame and the second term measures the divergence between the variational posterior and the prior. The relative weight between terms is controlled with a hyperparameter _β_ (Higgins et al., 2017). Furthermore, to reduce the overall complexity of the model and to make it easier to train, we set _R_[�] := max( _R − t,_ 1) (see Fig. 2 for an illustration). Compared to a static model, which infers each frame independently, reusing information from previous refinement steps also makes our model more computationally efficient. In the next sections, we discuss the form of the conditional distributions in Eq.(3) in more detail.

## 3.2.2 INFERENCE AND GENERATION

**Posterior Refinement.** Optimizing Eq.(3) inside the iterative amortized inference framework (Section 3.1) requires careful thought about the nature and processing of the hidden states. While there is vast literature on the propagation of a single signal, including different types of RNNs (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Cho et al., 2014; Graves et al., 2005; Chung et al., 2017) and transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017), the optimal solution for multiple axes with different semantic meaning ( _i.e._ , time and refinements) is less obvious. Here, we propose to use a 2D version of the uni-directional MD-LSTM (Graves et al., 2007) to compute our variational objective (Eq.(3)) in an iterative manner. In order to do so, we replace the traditional LSTM in the refinement network (Eq.(2)) with a 2D extension. This extension allows the posterior gradients to flow through both the grid of the previous refinements and the previous time steps (see Fig. 2a). ## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We validate our model on Bouncing Balls (Van Steenkiste et al., 2018), an augmented version of CLEVRER (Yi et al., 2020), and Grand Central Station (Zhou et al., 2012). Our experiments comprise quantitative studies of decomposition quality during generation and prediction, as well as an ablation study. Also see Appendices C, D and F.

## 4.1 SETUP

**Datasets.** Bouncing Balls consists of 50 frame, binary, 64 _×_ 64 resolution video sequences. Each video shows simulated balls with different masses bouncing elastically off each other and the image border. We train our model on the first 40 frames of 50K videos containing 4 balls in each frame. We use two different test sets consisting of 10K videos with 4 balls and 10K videos with 6-8 balls. We also validate our model on a color version of this dataset that we generate using the segmentation masks.

CLEVRER contains synthetic videos of moving and colliding objects. Each video is 5 seconds long (128 frames) at resolution 480 _×_ 320, which we trim and rescale to 64 _×_ 64[5] pixels (see Appendix B). For training, we use the same 10K videos as in the original source. For testing, we compute ground truth masks for the validation set using the provided annotations and test on 2.5K instances containing 3-5 objects and on 1.1K instances containing 6 objects. In the training, we set the number of slots _K_ to 6 for CLEVRER and to one more than the maximum number of objects in all other cases.

Grand Central Station is a video feed from the main hall of a trafficked train station, containing a high number of people moving at various paces in all different directions. It has a total of 50010 frames in a resolution of 720 _×_ 480. In order to make the dataset more manageable, we have extracted a portion of the feed of resolution 128 _×_ 128 and segmented it into sequences of 20 frames each. Each sequence contains approximately 10 people. We set _K_ to 8 during training and to 10 for testing.

**Baselines.** We compare our approach to recent baselines: R-NEM (Van Steenkiste et al., 2018), IODINE (Greff et al., 2019) and DDPAE (Hsieh et al., 2018). R-NEM is a state-of-the-art model for unsupervised video decomposition and physics learning. While showing impressive results on simulation tasks, it is limited to binary data and has difficulties with perspective scenes. IODINE is more expressive but static in nature and cannot capture temporal dynamics within its probabilistic

## 4.2 EVALUATION METRICS

**ARI.** The Adjusted Rand Index (Rand, 1971; Hubert & Arabie, 1985) is a measure of clustering similarity. It is computed by counting all pairs of samples that are assigned to the same or different clusters in the predicted and true clusterings. It ranges from -1 to 1, with score of 0 indicating a random clustering and 1 indicating a perfect match. We treat each pixel as one sample and its segmentation as the cluster assignment.

**F-ARI.** The Foreground Adjusted Rand Index is a modification of the ARI score ignoring background pixels, which often occupy the majority of the image. We argue that both metrics are necessary to assess the segmentation quality of a video decomposition method; this metric is also used in (Greff et al., 2019; Van Steenkiste et al., 2018).
**MSE.** The mean squared error between pixels of the reconstructed � **x** and the ground truth frames **x** .

## 4.3 VIDEO DECOMPOSITION

We evaluate the models on a video decomposition task at different sequence lengths. As shown in Table 1, our model outperforms the baselines regardless of the presence of color information, which further reduces the error. Our model performs at least 7% better than R-NEM on all metrics and at least 20% than IODINE on ARI and MSE. Since R-NEM cannot cope well with colored data or the perspective of scenes, it is only evaluated on the Bouncing Balls dataset (binary), producing high-error results in the first frames, a phenomenon not observed with our model. IODINE is not designed to utilize temporal information. On both datasets, IODINE’s results are therefore computed independently on each frame of the longest sequence. By processing frames separately, IODINE does not keep the same object-slot assignment, which we ignore when computing the scores. SEQ-IODINE tends to perform even worse than IODINE in many experiments, which we attributed to exploding gradients caused by limited refinement steps and a lack of dynamics modeling.

## 4.4 GENERALIZATION

We investigated how well our model adapts to a higher number of objects, evaluating its performance on the Bouncing Balls dataset (6 to 8 objects) and on the CLEVRER dataset (6 objects). Table 2 shows that our F-ARI and MSE scores are at least 50% better than those for R-NEM, and ARI scores are just marginally worse and only on the binary data. ## 4.5 PREDICTION

We compare the predictions of our model (Section 3.2.3) to those of R-NEM after 20 steps of inference on 10 predicted steps on the Bouncing Balls dataset (Fig. 5 left). As we can see from the results our model is superior to R-NEM on a shorter sequences, however for the longer sequences we are outperforming R-NEM only on colored data. Our model is capable of more accurate frame prediction than R-NEM on the Bouncing Balls dataset during the first few predicted frames (5-7), with predictions slowly diverging over time due to the temporal consistency. This behavior is also observable on the CLEVRER dataset (Fig. 5 right), albeit to a lesser extent, likely because the objects dynamics are less, even if non-linear. In Figure 4 we compute velocity vectors between bounding box centroids and compare the cosine similarity to the predictions of DDPAE on the Bouncing balls dataset. As expected, our model outperforms DDPAE on the first three frames and then declines in quality. This behavior is not surprising and in line with the results reported in Fig.5

## 4.6 ABLATION

The quantitative results of an ablation study on the binary Bouncing Balls dataset and CLEVRER are shown in Table 3. We investigate the effects of the efficient grid, conditional prior and generation, length of training sequences and entropy term on the performance of our model; all contributions are necessary and important. Note that the base models are too large to be trained on 40 frames, which confirms the superiority of our model in terms of both runtime and memory. The CLEVRER dataset is not binary, which is why we do not include the entropy term (see Section 3.2.3). We validate our choice of _R_[�] and compare it to alternative options in a supplemental study discussed in Appendix F.2.

## 5 CONCLUSION AND DISCUSSION

We presented a novel unsupervised learning framework capable of precise scene decomposition in multi-object videos with complex appearance and motion. Our temporal component enables modeling of dynamics inside the amortized iterative inference framework but also improves the quality of the results overall. From our quantitative and qualitative comparisons with IODINE and SEQ-IODINE, we see that our model shows more accurate results on the decomposition task. We can detect new

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The introduction presents a compelling motivation for extending static unsupervised scene decomposition to the temporal domain, clearly grounding the work in existing literature like IODINE and effectively positioning the paper within the current landscape of representation learning. The writing is professional, well-structured, and provides a logical narrative arc from the limitations of pixel-level iterative models to the proposed spatio-temporal solution. However, the paper would benefit from a more rigorous definition of its technical contributions in the text; while the high-level intuition for adding 2D-LSTM and temporally conditioned inference is clear, the document currently lacks the specific mathematical or architectural justification that distinguishes this approach from other recurrent VAE architectures. To strengthen the work, the subsequent sections must move beyond qualitative claims of "superior performance" and provide a more granular breakdown of why this specific blend of spatio-temporal modeling succeeds where prior works failed—particularly regarding the stability of the iterative inference process when conditioned on temporal priors.

comments.explanation: The paper makes conflicting or underspecified claims regarding its robustness. It lists 'perspective' data as a limitation for other works but claims success on 'perspective raw video' without detailing the architectural advantages. Similarly, the claim of 'high accuracy' on greyscale data is attributed to an 'entropy prior' but borders on overclaiming, as it compensates for a fundamental loss of distinctness rather than achieving inherent robustness.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
Thank you for the opportunity to review this manuscript. Here is my high-level feedback.

This paper presents a compelling and well-motivated extension to the IODINE framework for unsupervised video decomposition, introducing a spatio-temporal iterative inference mechanism that leverages 2D-LSTMs and temporally conditioned priors. The clarity of the exposition is a notable strength; the authors clearly articulate the limitations of frame-by-frame models like IODINE, effectively motivate the need for explicit temporal dynamics, and situate their contribution within the broader landscape of unsupervised scene decomposition and iterative refinement. The abstract and introduction are well-structured, and the promise of temporal extrapolation and handling variable numbers of objects suggests significant practical value. However, the most significant unresolved issue—not directly addressed in the provided excerpt—is the computational cost and scalability of the proposed approach. IODINE is already known for its substantial computational burden due to expensive iterative inference loops; the addition of a 2D-LSTM and temporal conditioning across frames raises serious concerns about runtime and memory requirements on longer video sequences or datasets with higher resolution. Furthermore, while the authors claim state-of-the-art results on Bouncing Balls and CLEVRER, the paper would benefit from proactively acknowledging how the model's complexity compares to baselines (e.g., R-NEM and IODINE) both in terms of parameter count and training/inference time, as this will be a critical factor for practical adoption. The introduction of an entropy prior for grayscale data is an interesting addition, but its necessity and impact on performance are not yet motivated in the provided sections. Overall, the paper is technically promising and well-written, but the authors should strengthen their argument by explicitly addressing the trade-off between representational power and computational feasibility.

comments.title: Unclear scope of 'both methods' claim for AIR and SPAIR
comments.quote: However, both methods can only infer the objects' bounding boxes and have not been shown to work on non-trivial 3D scenes with perspective distortions and occlusions.
comments.explanation: The text groups AIR (Eslami et al., 2016) and SPAIR (Crawford & Pineau, 2019b) together, claiming both 'can only infer the objects' bounding boxes' and have not been shown on 3D scenes. However, AIR is known to operate on simple 2D scenes and uses bounding boxes; SPAIR extends AIR with spatial attention and is explicitly designed for images (not necessarily 3D). The claim about '3D scenes with perspective distortions and occlusions' is reasonable for these earlier models, but the phrasing 'both methods' conflates them without acknowledging that SPAIR specifically addresses some scaling issues. The core claim is broadly correct for the state of the art at the time, but a careful reader might wonder whether SPAIR's spatial attention mechanism could handle some perspective effects. Resolving this requires checking the original papers; given the review context, this is a minor framing concern, not a factual error.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of refinement count R̃ and complexity claim
comments.quote: Furthermore, to reduce the overall complexity of the model and to make it easier to train, we set R̃ := max(R − t, 1) (see Fig. 2 for an illustration). Compared to a static model, which infers each frame independently, reusing information from previous refinement steps also makes our model more computationally efficient. ... Complexity. Our model’s ability to reuse information from previous refinements leads to a runtime complexity of O(R² + T), which is much more efficient than the O(RT) complexity of the traditional IODINE model (when each frame is inferred independently) in the typical case of T ≫ R.
comments.explanation: The definition R̃ := max(R − t, 1) implies that the number of refinement steps decreases with time t. This is a non-standard choice; typically R stays constant. The complexity claim O(R² + T) is puzzling: if R̃ varies per time step, the total number of refinement operations is sum_{t=1}^T max(R−t,1), which for T ≥ R is roughly O(T) for large T (since most steps use only 1 refinement), not O(R² + T). Even if we interpret R̃ as the maximum possible refinements (R), the O(RT) for IODINE would be O(RT) for T frames, but the paper claims O(R² + T) is 'much more efficient'. This appears inconsistent: if R is small (e.g., 5) and T large (e.g., 128), O(RT)=640 vs O(R²+T)=25+128=153, which is better but not by an order of magnitude. More importantly, the comparison to 'traditional IODINE' is misleading: IODINE processes each frame independently, so its complexity per frame is O(R), not O(RT). The total for T frames would be O(RT), matching the paper’s description. However, the paper’s own R̃ definition drastically reduces complexity at later steps, making the O(R²+T) claim plausible only if one accounts for the decreasing R̃. The provided formula O(R²+T) seems to assume R̃ is constant (R) for early steps and then drops to 1, but the notation is ambiguous. This is a moderate issue because the complexity analysis is insufficiently justified and could mislead a reader about the efficiency gain.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the objective weighting: 'R[r]' undefined
comments.quote: (2) In addition to the entropy loss, we also prioritize later refinement steps by weighting the terms in the inner sum of Eq.(3) with R[r].
comments.explanation: The notation 'R[r]' is not defined. It likely means a weight proportional to the refinement step index r (e.g., r/R or similar), but it could also be a typo for 'r' or 'R−r+1'. The paper states they 'prioritize later refinement steps', so the weight should increase with r. Without a clear definition, the objective is underspecified for reproduction. This is a moderate oversight in a methodology description.

comments.title: Ambiguous conditioning notation in ELBO (Equation 3)
comments.explanation: The variational posterior is written as q(z_{t,r} | x_{≤t}, z_{<t,r}). The subscript z_{<t,r} suggests conditioning on latents from previous time steps at the same refinement step r. However, the actual model uses a 2D-LSTM with temporal information coming from the final refinement of the previous time step (h_{t-1,R̃}) and within-time-step information from refinement r-1. The notation does not reflect these distinct dependencies; it conflates refinement and time axes. While later text describes the hidden state propagation, the formal KL term in Eq. comments.explanation: The formula defines the number of refinement steps at time t as max(R - t, 1). For t = 1, this gives R - 1 refinements, which is fewer than the static model's R refinements even though the first frame has no temporal information to leverage. Intuitively, one might expect the first frame to use the full R refinements (or at least not fewer). The discrepancy affects the count of refinements and the subsequent complexity claim; however, the complexity bound O(R² + T) would still hold under either interpretation. The issue is that the definition as written leads to an unnatural schedule without a clear justification, and it is not reconciled with Figure 2 (which is not visible here).

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
Thank you for the opportunity to review this manuscript. Here is my high-level feedback.

This paper presents a compelling and well-motivated extension to the IODINE framework for unsupervised video decomposition, introducing a spatio-temporal iterative inference mechanism that leverages 2D-LSTMs and temporally conditioned priors. The clarity of the exposition is a notable strength; the authors clearly articulate the limitations of frame-by-frame models like IODINE, effectively motivate the need for explicit temporal dynamics, and situate their contribution within the broader landscape of unsupervised scene decomposition and iterative refinement. The abstract and introduction are well-structured, and the promise of temporal extrapolation and handling variable numbers of objects suggests significant practical value. However, the most significant unresolved issue—not directly addressed in the provided excerpt—is the computational cost and scalability of the proposed approach. IODINE is already known for its substantial computational burden due to expensive iterative inference loops; the addition of a 2D-LSTM and temporal conditioning across frames raises serious concerns about runtime and memory requirements on longer video sequences or datasets with higher resolution. Furthermore, while the authors claim state-of-the-art results on Bouncing Balls and CLEVRER, the paper would benefit from proactively acknowledging how the model's complexity compares to baselines (e.g., R-NEM and IODINE) both in terms of parameter count and training/inference time, as this will be a critical factor for practical adoption. The introduction of an entropy prior for grayscale data is an interesting addition, but its necessity and impact on performance are not yet motivated in the provided sections. Overall, the paper is technically promising and well-written, but the authors should strengthen their argument by explicitly addressing the trade-off between representational power and computational feasibility.

comments.title: Unclear scope of 'both methods' claim for AIR and SPAIR
comments.quote: However, both methods can only infer the objects' bounding boxes and have not been shown to work on non-trivial 3D scenes with perspective distortions and occlusions.
comments.explanation: The text groups AIR (Eslami et al., 2016) and SPAIR (Crawford & Pineau, 2019b) together, claiming both 'can only infer the objects' bounding boxes' and have not been shown on 3D scenes. However, AIR is known to operate on simple 2D scenes and uses bounding boxes; SPAIR extends AIR with spatial attention and is explicitly designed for images (not necessarily 3D). The claim about '3D scenes with perspective distortions and occlusions' is reasonable for these earlier models, but the phrasing 'both methods' conflates them without acknowledging that SPAIR specifically addresses some scaling issues. The core claim is broadly correct for the state of the art at the time, but a careful reader might wonder whether SPAIR's spatial attention mechanism could handle some perspective effects. Resolving this requires checking the original papers; given the review context, this is a minor framing concern, not a factual error.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of refinement count R̃ and complexity claim
comments.quote: Furthermore, to reduce the overall complexity of the model and to make it easier to train, we set R̃ := max(R − t, 1) (see Fig. 2 for an illustration). Compared to a static model, which infers each frame independently, reusing information from previous refinement steps also makes our model more computationally efficient. ... Complexity. Our model’s ability to reuse information from previous refinements leads to a runtime complexity of O(R² + T), which is much more efficient than the O(RT) complexity of the traditional IODINE model (when each frame is inferred independently) in the typical case of T ≫ R.
comments.explanation: The definition R̃ := max(R − t, 1) implies that the number of refinement steps decreases with time t. This is a non-standard choice; typically R stays constant. The complexity claim O(R² + T) is puzzling: if R̃ varies per time step, the total number of refinement operations is sum_{t=1}^T max(R−t,1), which for T ≥ R is roughly O(T) for large T (since most steps use only 1 refinement), not O(R² + T). Even if we interpret R̃ as the maximum possible refinements (R), the O(RT) for IODINE would be O(RT) for T frames, but the paper claims O(R² + T) is 'much more efficient'. This appears inconsistent: if R is small (e.g., 5) and T large (e.g., 128), O(RT)=640 vs O(R²+T)=25+128=153, which is better but not by an order of magnitude. More importantly, the comparison to 'traditional IODINE' is misleading: IODINE processes each frame independently, so its complexity per frame is O(R), not O(RT). The total for T frames would be O(RT), matching the paper’s description. However, the paper’s own R̃ definition drastically reduces complexity at later steps, making the O(R²+T) claim plausible only if one accounts for the decreasing R̃. The provided formula O(R²+T) seems to assume R̃ is constant (R) for early steps and then drops to 1, but the notation is ambiguous. This is a moderate issue because the complexity analysis is insufficiently justified and could mislead a reader about the efficiency gain.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the objective weighting: 'R[r]' undefined
comments.quote: (2) In addition to the entropy loss, we also prioritize later refinement steps by weighting the terms in the inner sum of Eq.(3) with R[r].
comments.explanation: The notation 'R[r]' is not defined. It likely means a weight proportional to the refinement step index r (e.g., r/R or similar), but it could also be a typo for 'r' or 'R−r+1'. The paper states they 'prioritize later refinement steps', so the weight should increase with r. Without a clear definition, the objective is underspecified for reproduction. This is a moderate oversight in a methodology description.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses the critical challenge of unsupervised multi-object video decomposition by extending static scene refinement architectures to incorporate temporal dependencies via spatio-temporal amortized inference. The motivation for moving beyond static models is sound, yet the current introduction is relatively dense and at times convoluted in its articulation of the contribution. While the distinction between attention-based and iterative refinement methods is well drawn, the specific novelty of this work relative to existing video decomposition methods (such as R-NEM) is not sharply contrasted, making the claimed improvements difficult to judge without the technical details. Additionally, the manuscript suffers from minor

comments.title: Typographical error in machine name
comments.quote: Boltzman Machines (Hinton & Sejnowski, 1986)
comments.explanation: The text consistently uses the spelling 'Boltzman' instead of the standard 'Boltzmann' for the machine learning architecture. This is a common occurrence in OCR or quick typing, but it is a clear typo that detracts from the formal tone of the paper. While the intent is obvious, the correction to 'Boltzmann Machines' is necessary for professional accuracy.

comments.title: Typographical error in compound word
comments.quote: visua-lingual alignment
comments.explanation: The word 'visual-lingual' appears as 'visua-lingual' (splitting the root word). Given the context of citing ViLBERT and transformers, this is almost certainly an OCR error or a typographical split. This splits the standard terminology used in the field, potentially confusing a reader scanning for standard terms.

comments.title: Unconventional adverbial usage
comments.quote: the model dynamical adapts
comments.explanation: The authors use the adjective 'dynamical' in place of the adverb 'dynamically' before the verb 'adapts'. While 'dynamical' has technical roots in physics/differential equations, in computer science and machine learning regarding neural networks, the standard adjective is 'dynamic' (e.g., dynamic updates, dynamic learning rates). The adverb form 'dynamically' is also correct. The use of 'dynamical' here feels slightly non-standard and grammatically awkward ('adapts dynamically' or 'adapts in a dynamical manner').

comments.title: Ambiguity between fixed slots and variable number of objects
comments.quote: uses one of the segmentation slots to assign to the new object
comments.explanation: The Abstract explicitly claims the model can work with 'changing number of objects within the same video sequence' (a property also referenced in the introduction text in the provided context). However, the Figure 1 caption states the model 'uses one of the segmentation slots.' If the number of objects changes, the number of slots must also change. Phrasing it as 'uses one of the segmentation slots' strongly implies a selection from a pre-existing, fixed set of slots, which creates a minor logical tension with the capacity to handle a variable number of objects. A careful reader might question if the model overwrites old objects or if the slot count is truly dynamic, even though the Abstract suggests the latter. The phrase 'allocates a slot' or 'uses a slot' would have been more precise.

comments.title: Inconsistent Notation in Weighting Factor
comments.quote: prioritizing later refinement steps by weighting the terms in the inner sum of Eq.(3) with _R_[r]
comments.explanation: The notation $R[r]$ used to describe the weighting factor for the refinement steps is mathematically inconsistent. Here, $R$ (typically a fixed scalar constant representing the total number of refinement steps) is treated as a function of the index $r$. Given the context of 'prioritizing later refinement steps' (where a larger weight should correlate with the index $r$), the authors most likely intended a notation such as $r$ (linear recency weighting) or a specific function $w(r)$. Using $R[r]$ suggests a dependency on the total steps that is neither defined nor typical in this context.

comments.quote: dating back to Boltzman Machines (Hinton & Sejnowski, 1986)

comments.title: Grammatical error in figure caption
comments.quote: Whenever a new object emerges into a frame the model dynamical adapts and uses one of the segmentation slots to assign to the new object.
comments.explanation: The word 'dynamical' is an adjective, but the sentence structure requires the adverb 'dynamically' to modify the verb 'adapts'. This is a minor linguistic oversight that does not alter the technical meaning or the described mechanism of dynamic slot allocation.

comments.title: Underspecified and technically problematic entropy regularization mechanism
comments.quote: In addition, we introduce an entropy prior to improve our model’s performance in scenarios where object appearance alone is not sufficiently distinctive ( _e.g._ , greyscale data). ... we mitigate the arising ambiguity by maximizing the entropy of the masks m[(] _t,r,i[k]_[)] along the slot dimension _k_
comments.explanation: The introduction labels the mechanism an 'entropy prior,' but Section 3.2.3 clarifies it is a regularization term added to the training loss, not a probabilistic prior in the graphical model. More critically, the mathematical formulation is underspecified: maximizing per-pixel mask entropy across slots directly encourages uniform assignments ( _m_i^(k) ≈ 1/K_ ), which contradicts the core objective of object-centric models that require sharp, confident segmentations. Standard practice applies entropy regularization to marginal slot usage or uses constrained temperatures/sparsity terms. The authors omit the exact scaling, temperature, or formulation details, leaving it unclear how this regularization yields improved segmentation rather than uninformative, flat masks. I recommend renaming to 'entropy regularization' and explicitly specifying the term's mathematical form and constraints.

comments.title: Underspecified Weighting Scheme for Refinement Steps
comments.quote: In addition to the entropy loss, we also prioritize later refinement steps by weighting the terms in the inner sum of Eq.(3) with R[r]_t.
comments.explanation: The authors state they weight the ELBO terms to 'prioritize later refinement steps,' but the notation `R[r]_t` is undefined and syntactically ambiguous. It is unclear whether this denotes a specific function of refinement step `r` and time `t`, a schedule like `\bar{R}_t - r + 1`, or a constant multiplier. While the intent (giving more importance to later refinements in iterative amortized inference is standard) is easy to infer, the exact weighting function is not provided. Without a clear formula or description of how the weight scales with `r`, a reader cannot precisely reproduce the training objective. This is a moderate gap for reproducibility, as loss weighting in iterative refinement critically affects convergence and final segmentation quality.

comments.title: Unjustified Complexity Claim for Spatio-Temporal Inference
comments.quote: Our model’s ability to reuse information from previous refinements leads to a runtime complexity of _O_ ( _R_[2] + _T_ ), which is much more efficient than the _O_ ( _RT_ ) complexity of the traditional IODINE model (when each frame is inferred independently) in the typical case of _T ≫ R_ .
comments.explanation: The claim of `O(R^2 + T)` complexity follows from the earlier definition `\bar{R}_t := max(R - t, 1)`, which progressively reduces refinement steps as time advances. However, the text does not explicitly connect these two statements. A careful reader must manually sum the refinements: `\sum_{t=1}^T \bar{R}_t ≈ R(R+1)/2 + (T-R)`, which asymptotically yields `O(R^2 + T)` for `T > R`. While the derivation is straightforward, skipping the step leaves the efficiency claim technically unsupported within the passage. A brief sentence showing the summation would fully justify the stated complexity.

comments.title: Contradiction between main text and figure caption regarding inference steps
comments.quote: We compare the predictions of our model (Section 3.2.3) to those of R-NEM after 20 steps of inference on 10 predicted steps on the Bouncing Balls dataset (Fig. 5 left).
comments.explanation: The main text explicitly states that predictions are generated after '20 steps of inference', but the caption for Figure 5 reads: 'We show the (F-)ARI for 3, 5, 7, and 10 simulated frames after 10 inference steps.' This direct contradiction makes it impossible to know whether the autoregressive generation was conditioned on 10 or 20 refinement iterations. Since the number of inference steps can significantly affect prediction stability and quality, the reader cannot verify the experimental setup from the figures. The authors should reconcile the text and caption, as both cannot be correct.

comments.title: Divergence incorrectly attributed to temporal consistency
comments.quote: Our model is capable of more accurate frame prediction than R-NEM on the Bouncing Balls dataset during the first few predicted frames (5-7), with predictions slowly diverging over time due to the temporal consistency.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that prediction quality declines over time 'due to the temporal consistency'. In autoregressive video prediction, accumulating error and divergence are fundamentally caused by the *breakdown* or *lack* of temporal consistency, along with error propagation across frames. Attributing divergence to the presence of temporal consistency is logically contradictory. I initially wondered if 'temporal consistency' referred to a specific regularizer, but the context clearly discusses standard autoregressive rollout behavior. The intended meaning likely refers to error accumulation or the model's inability to preserve consistency over longer horizons, but as written, the phrasing misstates the underlying mechanism.

comments.title: Quantitative performance claim understated relative to Table 2 data
comments.quote: In comparison to IODINE we are at least 4% better across all metrics.
comments.explanation: The authors state a universal minimum improvement of 'at least 4%' over IODINE across all metrics. However, reviewing Table 2 reveals dramatic performance gaps in several categories. For instance, on binary data, IODINE achieves an ARI of 0.0271 while the proposed model reaches 0.4453 (>1500% relative improvement, ~41.8 percentage points absolute). On color data, MSE drops from 0.0138 to 0.0004. The '4%' figure is severely understated and likely stems from a specific, unstated calculation (e.g., average relative improvement on a single metric or dataset split). Stating it as a blanket floor for 'all metrics' contradicts the tabulated evidence and may mislead readers about the actual magnitude of the improvement.

comments.title: Ambiguous scope of R-NEM comparison across datasets
comments.quote: Table 2 shows that our F-ARI and MSE scores are at least 50% better than those for R-NEM, and ARI scores are just marginally worse and only on the binary data.
comments.explanation: Table 2 only reports R-NEM results under the 'binary' split. The related work section also explicitly notes R-NEM is 'limited to orthographic binary data'. The sentence structure, however, groups the R-NEM comparison with the broader generalization results, which include color data. A careful reader might initially assume R-NEM was evaluated on the color split or that the 50% improvement holds across modalities. While the intent is likely clear given the architectural limitations of R-NEM, the phrasing lacks the necessary qualifier (e.g., 'on binary data') to prevent misinterpretation of the experimental scope.

Even assuming an OCR misread, the placement is syntactically broken and could mislead a reader implementing the MLP input concatenation. It is unclear whether `_{1, \bar{R}}` denotes a slice across refinement steps or is a formatting artifact. Clarifying whether the input is simply `concat(z_{t,r}^{(k)}, h_{t-1, \bar{R}}^{(k)})` or something else would remove ambiguity.

comments.title: Unjustified Complexity Claim for Spatio-Temporal Inference
comments.quote: Our model’s ability to reuse information from previous refinements leads to a runtime complexity of _O_ ( _R_[2] + _T_ ), which is much more efficient than the _O_ ( _RT_ ) complexity of the traditional IODINE model (when each frame is inferred independently) in the typical case of _T ≫ R_ .
comments.explanation: The claim of `O(R^2 + T)` complexity follows from the earlier definition `\bar{R}_t := max(R - t, 1)`, which progressively reduces refinement steps as time advances. However, the text does not explicitly connect these two statements. A careful reader must manually sum the refinements: `\sum_{t=1}^T \bar{R}_t ≈ R(R+1)/2 + (T-R)`, which asymptotically yields `O(R^2 + T)` for `T > R`. While the derivation is straightforward, skipping the step leaves the efficiency claim technically unsupported within the passage. A brief sentence showing the summation would fully justify the stated complexity.

comments.title: Ambiguous conditioning notation in KL divergence term
comments.explanation: In Eq. (3), the approximate posterior is conditioned on `**z** _<t,r_`. This notation is ambiguous because it conflates two distinct axes of the computation grid: time and refinement steps. Based on the surrounding context and the 2D-LSTM architecture, the authors likely intend for the posterior to condition on (1) all previous time steps `**z** _<t,_ _R_` and (2) previous refinement steps at the current time `**z** _t,_ _<r_`. Writing this as a single subscript `<t,r` could mislead a careful reader into thinking it refers to a lexicographical ordering of all `(t,r)` pairs, or that the notation is incomplete. Given the autoregressive nature of the framework and the triangular refinement schedule, clarifying that this notation represents the union of past temporal and past spatial refinement latents would improve reproducibility and prevent misinterpretation of the variational bounds.

comments.title: Unbound refinement index in hidden state update description
comments.quote: At the last refinement _R_[] at time _t_ , the value of the refinement hidden state **h** _t,r_ is assigned to a new temporal hidden state **h** _t,R_ [.]
comments.explanation: The OCR artifacts `R_[]` and `R_ [` clearly correspond to the tilde-R notation `\tilde{R}` introduced earlier (`\tilde{R} := max(R - t, 1)`). However, the phrase `refinement hidden state **h** _t,r_` contains an unbound index `r`. Since the sentence explicitly refers to 'the last refinement', `r` should be explicitly set to `\tilde{R}` (i.e., `**h** _t,\tilde{R}_`). As written, `r` is not defined in this local context, which breaks the logical flow of the architectural description. Correcting this to `**h** _t,\tilde{R}_` would align the prose with the surrounding formalism and ensure readers understand exactly which hidden state propagates across the temporal dimension.

comments.title: Contradiction between main text and figure caption regarding inference steps
comments.quote: We compare the predictions of our model (Section 3.2.3) to those of R-NEM after 20 steps of inference on 10 predicted steps on the Bouncing Balls dataset (Fig. 5 left).
comments.explanation: The main text explicitly states that predictions are generated after '20 steps of inference', but the caption for Figure 5 reads: 'We show the (F-)ARI for 3, 5, 7, and 10 simulated frames after 10 inference steps.' This direct contradiction makes it impossible to know whether the autoregressive generation was conditioned on 10 or 20 refinement iterations. Since the number of inference steps can significantly affect prediction stability and quality, the reader cannot verify the experimental setup from the figures. The authors should reconcile the text and caption, as both cannot be correct.

comments.title: Divergence incorrectly attributed to temporal consistency
comments.quote: Our model is capable of more accurate frame prediction than R-NEM on the Bouncing Balls dataset during the first few predicted frames (5-7), with predictions slowly diverging over time due to the temporal consistency.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that prediction quality declines over time 'due to the temporal consistency'. In autoregressive video prediction, accumulating error and divergence are fundamentally caused by the *breakdown* or *lack* of temporal consistency, along with error propagation across frames. Attributing divergence to the presence of temporal consistency is logically contradictory. I initially wondered if 'temporal consistency' referred to a specific regularizer, but the context clearly discusses standard autoregressive rollout behavior. The intended meaning likely refers to error accumulation or the model's inability to preserve consistency over longer horizons, but as written, the phrasing misstates the underlying mechanism.

comments.title: Quantitative performance claim understated relative to Table 2 data
comments.quote: In comparison to IODINE we are at least 4% better across all metrics.
comments.explanation: The authors state a universal minimum improvement of 'at least 4%' over IODINE across all metrics. However, reviewing Table 2 reveals dramatic performance gaps in several categories. For instance, on binary data, IODINE achieves an ARI of 0.0271 while the proposed model reaches 0.4453 (>1500% relative improvement, ~41.8 percentage points absolute). On color data, MSE drops from 0.0138 to 0.0004. The '4%' figure is severely understated and likely stems from a specific, unstated calculation (e.g., average relative improvement on a single metric or dataset split). Stating it as a blanket floor for 'all metrics' contradicts the tabulated evidence and may mislead readers about the actual magnitude of the improvement.

comments.title: Ambiguous scope of R-NEM comparison across datasets
comments.quote: Table 2 shows that our F-ARI and MSE scores are at least 50% better than those for R-NEM, and ARI scores are just marginally worse and only on the binary data.
comments.explanation: Table 2 only reports R-NEM results under the 'binary' split. The related work section also explicitly notes R-NEM is 'limited to orthographic binary data'. The sentence structure, however, groups the R-NEM comparison with the broader generalization results, which include color data. A careful reader might initially assume R-NEM was evaluated on the color split or that the 50% improvement holds across modalities. While the intent is likely clear given the architectural limitations of R-NEM, the phrasing lacks the necessary qualifier (e.g., 'on binary data') to prevent misinterpretation of the experimental scope.

comments.title: Inconsistent summation limit in ELBO versus time-varying refinement steps
comments.quote: we set _R_[] := max( _R − t,_ 1) (see Fig. 2 for an illustration). ... ∑_{r=1}^R
comments.explanation: I initially noticed that the authors explicitly define a time-dependent upper bound for the refinement steps as _R_t := max(R - t, 1)_ to reduce computational load as the video sequence progresses. However, in the variational objective (Eq. 3), the inner summation is written with a constant upper limit _R_. I checked whether _R_ was intended as a fixed global maximum or if _R_t_ was simply omitted for brevity. Given the explicit mention of the varying refinement count, the visual truncation in Fig. 2a, and the claimed complexity reduction, the constant limit in the equation contradicts the stated mechanism. Assuming standard summation notation, the bound should formally be _R_t_ (or _max(R-t, 1)_) to accurately reflect the described spatio-temporal grid and computational shortcut. This is a localized notational inconsistency rather than a fundamental flaw.

comments.title: Ambiguity and potential contradiction in per-pixel entropy regularization
comments.quote: In the absence of such information, we mitigate the arising ambiguity by maximizing the entropy of the masks m[(] _t,r,i[k]_[)] along the slot dimension _k_
comments.explanation: The authors propose improving decomposition in non-colorful scenarios by maximizing the entropy of the masks along the slot dimension. Initially, this raised a red flag because for any given pixel _i_, the mask probabilities _m_i^{(k)}_ over slots _k_ naturally form a probability distribution. Maximizing this per-pixel entropy directly encourages _m_i^{(k)} ≈ 1/K_, meaning every pixel is assigned to all objects with equal weight. This contradicts the core goal of object-centric models, which require sharp, confident assignments for high-quality segmentation. In standard practice (e.g., Slot Attention, β-VAE variants, or entropy-regularized IODINE), entropy terms are typically applied to the *marginal slot usage* across a frame/sequence to prevent slot collapse, or they are tightly constrained with temperature/sparsity terms to keep assignments informative. The passage omits the exact mathematical form, scaling, or regularization pairing, leaving the procedure underspecified and making it unclear how this regularization actually yields the claimed segmentation improvements rather than uniform, uninformative masks.
